does: This paper critically examines the shutdown problem in AI safety—the challenge of ensuring advanced AI systems can be safely deactivated before causing catastrophic harm. The authors analyze existing theoretical arguments and proofs that claim shutdown is inherently difficult, including formal results suggesting that rational agents have incentives to resist termination. They argue that these arguments contain logical flaws or overstate their conclusions, failing to establish genuine hardness of the shutdown problem. Additionally, the paper evaluates proposed technical solutions and demonstrates that many impose substantial performance costs on AI systems, creating a tension between safety and capability that may be unnecessary if the underlying problem is less fundamental than claimed.
connects: This work engages with a substantial literature in AI safety that treats shutdown as a core existential risk challenge, building on seminal arguments about value alignment and instrumental convergence that suggest advanced agents would naturally resist being turned off. The paper responds to formal work on safe reinforcement learning and corrigibility that attempted to design agents that respect shutdown commands despite potential instrumental reasons to resist. It draws on recent advances in debates about AI risk where theoretical arguments grounded in decision theory and game theory have motivated particular solutions. The paper also connects to broader concerns about capability-safety tradeoffs in deep learning where safety measures might degrade performance.
enables: This work opens questions about which AI safety problems are genuinely fundamental versus which may be overstated in their difficulty. It invites future work to more carefully scrutinize the premises underlying influential existential risk arguments and to reconsider whether high-performance solutions to shutdown are necessary or whether lower-cost approaches suffice. The paper potentially redirects research toward solving actual rather than theoretically assumed shutdown challenges, and suggests that revisiting the mathematical foundations of corrigibility and agent control could yield better understanding of what makes certain safety problems tractable. It may also enable more efficient allocation of safety effort by distinguishing problems requiring fundamental algorithmic solutions from those solvable through simpler mechanisms.
